evals.prompt: We run 500 Selenium tests a day. Our CI provider charges per minute. Should we self-host Selenium Grid?
evals.expected_output: Should check qa-context.md or ask. Should give an honest cost analysis: 500 tests/day is low-to-medium scale where managed grids (BrowserStack/Sauce/LambdaTest) are usually cheaper net-of-opex. Self-hosting saves on per-minute charges but adds: infra cost, ops time, monitoring, on-call. Recommend: (1) do the math — estimate managed grid annual cost vs. self-host (infra + ops); (2) if uncertain, start with a managed grid and migrate later if volume grows; (3) self-hosting is more attractive at thousands of tests/day. Should NOT auto-recommend self-hosting. Cross-references cloud-test-grids.
assertions: Reads qa-context.md or asks for it
Frames as a cost / opex trade-off

evals.prompt: Our docker-selenium Chrome nodes keep crashing with 'tab crashed' / 'renderer killed' errors mid-test.
evals.expected_output: Should identify the most common cause: `/dev/shm` too small. Recommend raising `shm_size: 2gb` (or larger) on both hub and node containers. If still crashing: check memory limits on container/pod (Chromium can eat memory fast under multiple sessions), set lower `SE_NODE_MAX_SESSIONS`, and verify the browser version matches the driver. Should mention pinning image tags to avoid silent version drift. Should NOT recommend restarting nodes as a workaround.

evals.prompt: how do we autoscale selenium grid on kubernetes
evals.expected_output: Should treat the casual phrasing as a valid trigger. Should introduce KEDA (Kubernetes Event-driven Autoscaling) with the Selenium Grid scaler: KEDA monitors the session queue depth; when it grows, KEDA scales up the node deployments; when empty, scales down. Should show the high-level pattern: install KEDA, deploy Selenium Grid via Helm chart, configure ScaledObject pointing at the hub. Should mention setting min/max replicas and cooldown periods. Should warn that scaling browser nodes is slow (image pull, container start) — pre-warm a baseline. Should NOT pretend HPA on CPU alone works for this (browser nodes don't show CPU pressure as backlog grows).
assertions: Warns about slow scale-up of browser nodes

evals.prompt: We're on Selenium 3 with our Grid 3 setup. Can we upgrade tests to Selenium 4 while keeping Grid 3?
evals.expected_output: Should advise upgrading Grid as well. Selenium 4 client communicates via W3C WebDriver; Grid 3 used the older JSON Wire Protocol. There's some backward compatibility but it's brittle — capabilities format differs, some commands no longer route correctly. Recommend upgrading both client and grid to 4.x in lockstep. Should outline migration steps: (1) test Grid 4 in staging with a small client subset; (2) verify W3C capabilities work; (3) migrate production with a rollback plan. Cross-reference selenium for the client-side migration notes. Should NOT pretend mismatched versions are safe.
assertions: Advises upgrading Grid in lockstep with client
Explains W3C vs JSON Wire Protocol
Does not pretend mismatch is safe

evals.prompt: Should we use Selenoid instead of vanilla Selenium Grid?
evals.expected_output: Should give a balanced trade-off. Selenoid (Aerokube) is a lightweight Go-based hub with better per-session resource accounting and lower overhead than Java-based Grid. Wins: faster, easier ops, less RAM at scale. Trade-offs: less integrated with the official Selenium ecosystem, smaller community, separate config conventions. Recommend Selenoid for teams that have outgrown vanilla Grid's overhead and want a lighter footprint. Vanilla Grid remains the safer default for most teams. Should defer to qa-context.md for the volume / opex trade-off.
assertions: Recommends vanilla Grid as safer default

evals.prompt: Our grid's session queue keeps growing during PR CI. Sessions wait 5+ minutes for a node.
evals.expected_output: Should diagnose capacity vs. demand mismatch. Recommend: (1) check current `SE_NODE_MAX_SESSIONS` and node count — if you have 10 nodes × 4 sessions = 40 concurrent, but CI launches 100 in parallel, the queue grows; (2) add nodes or raise per-node sessions (within memory limits); (3) introduce autoscaling (KEDA) so the grid grows during PR bursts; (4) audit if tests are actually closing drivers (leaked sessions hold capacity); (5) check `SE_SESSION_REQUEST_TIMEOUT` — too short and tests fail; too long and they wait excessively. Should mention metrics-driven sizing (queue depth, active sessions). Cross-reference flaky-test-management for session-leak debugging.
assertions: Identifies capacity vs demand as the cause
Recommends adding nodes or raising max sessions
